28:
Overview

This project implements a full teleoperation pipeline in the browser: a webcam-based computer vision system extracts 21 hand keypoints via MediaPipe, maps them to joint angles through a custom kinematic solver, and drives articulated URDF robotic hand models rendered in Three.js. It supports 31 different robotic hand models and runs at interactive frame rates with zero installation.

Approach

  • Built a real-time hand keypoint extraction pipeline using MediaPipe Hands, running inference on every webcam frame.
  • Implemented a hand-to-joint mapping layer that converts 3D keypoint positions into per-joint rotation angles compatible with URDF model structures (forward kinematics).
  • Loaded and parsed URDF files for 31 robotic hand models, each with different joint hierarchies and link geometries, using a custom URDF loader in Three.js.
  • Managed application state with React 18 and Zustand to keep the control loop responsive while the UI stays interactive.

Takeaway

This is the closest project to a real robotic teleoperation system: it has a perception layer (CV), a control mapping layer (keypoint-to-joint), and an actuation visualization layer (URDF rendering). The same architecture extends to driving physical robot hands over ROS or serial communication -- the browser visualization is a simulation-first approach to validating the control pipeline before hardware integration.
26:
Overview

Snapchat Glass Bridge Challenge is a turn-based multiplayer Lens Studio game inspired by the Glass Bridge sequence. The project focuses on asynchronous competition, where players take turns, compare attempts, and move through the same challenge via Snapchat messaging.

Approach

  • Built a custom game manager around Lens Studio's turn-based feature.
  • Used an event-driven handler pattern to keep gameplay systems separated.
  • Integrated Bitmoji SDK support so the experience feels native to Snapchat.

Takeaway

The main challenge was designing a game that still feels competitive when players are not on screen at the same time. The architecture was shaped around clear turn logic, message passing, and modular game stages that can grow into future async games.
29:
Overview

GoGreenNext - Modular Map Tiles is a Blender environment asset project created for an NYU game team. The work centers on reusable 3D tiles that make level building faster while keeping the world visually consistent.

Approach

  • Modeled a modular tile set with standardized dimensions and edge matching.
  • Designed the pieces so they can snap together cleanly in a grid-based workflow.
  • Built enough variation in terrain and structure pieces to support flexible level layouts.

Takeaway

The project is less about a single hero asset and more about the system behind the assets. Good modular design reduces friction for level design and makes it easier to build larger worlds without breaking visual continuity.
27:
Overview

CuraLoop implements a real-time AI monitoring pipeline with a Human-in-the-Loop architecture. The system continuously ingests behavioral signals (chat patterns, game performance metrics), runs AI inference to detect cognitive trends, and surfaces alerts to human reviewers. This sensor-to-insight-to-human-review loop mirrors robotic monitoring systems where autonomous perception feeds into human-supervised decision-making.

Approach

  • Built a data ingestion pipeline that captures behavioral signals from chat and game interactions in real time using React Native and Expo.
  • Implemented an AI inference layer with Claude Sonnet via FastAPI that processes signals and produces trend analysis without replacing human judgment.
  • Designed a dashboard for human reviewers to inspect AI-generated insights and override or confirm recommendations -- a Human-in-the-Loop pattern used in robotic fleet monitoring and autonomous system supervision.

Takeaway

The architecture -- continuous data capture, real-time AI inference, human oversight -- is the same pattern used in robotic telemonitoring and autonomous system supervision. The project demonstrates experience building closed-loop AI pipelines where the AI assists but a human remains in the decision loop.
23:
Overview

B612 Soccer implements a custom physics engine for a spherical world: radial gravity, wrap-around collision detection, and angular momentum on a curved surface. Built solo in 48 hours for GMTK Game Jam 2025 and ranked top 2% of 3,300+ entries. The non-Euclidean physics simulation involves the same vector math and coordinate transforms used in robotic path planning on non-planar surfaces.

Approach

  • Built a custom circular physics system in Unity/C# handling radial gravity, tangential forces, and wrap-around collisions on a spherical surface.
  • Implemented coordinate transforms between local surface frames and world space -- the same reference-frame math used in robotic kinematics and multi-body simulation.
  • Optimized the physics loop to maintain stable simulation at 60fps with multiple interacting rigid bodies under non-standard gravity.

Takeaway

Writing a physics engine from scratch -- especially one with non-standard gravity and curved-surface dynamics -- builds the same mathematical intuition needed for robotic simulation: rigid body dynamics, collision resolution, and coordinate frame transforms. The top 2% ranking validates that the simulation was both technically correct and performant under real-time constraints.
01:
Overview

Hardware Store Smart Search is a lead generation tool built for a hardware manufacturer that needed a better way to identify target stores. It turns Google Maps business data into a searchable pipeline for finding potential customers around the world.

Approach

  • Crawled business listings from map data and organized the results by location and relevance.
  • Added deduplication and caching so repeated searches stay useful and fast.
  • Used FastAPI on the backend and React on the frontend to keep the workflow simple.

Takeaway

The project is a practical example of productizing messy public data. The value comes from turning a broad search problem into a workflow that sales teams can actually use.
02:
Overview

Built during an internship at Snap Inc., this project implements a real-time perception-to-geometry pipeline. Hand tracking extracts finger keypoints from the camera feed, and a procedural mesh generator converts the tracked 3D trajectory into dynamic geometry -- creating volumetric drawings in AR space from hand motion alone. This is the same sensor-to-output architecture used in robotic end-effector path recording and trajectory playback systems.

Approach

  • Used Lens Studio's hand tracking API to extract finger joint positions at camera frame rate, providing continuous 3D pose data.
  • Built a procedural mesh generation system that constructs triangle strips along the tracked trajectory, with normal computation and UV mapping for proper rendering.
  • Implemented spatial filtering and smoothing to handle tracking jitter -- a common challenge in both AR and robotic perception pipelines.
  • Designed the system as a reusable module that other Lens Studio developers could integrate, which was published in official Snap documentation.

Takeaway

The core pipeline -- track hand pose, compute 3D path, generate output in real time -- mirrors robotic trajectory recording. The project shipped as a developer tool, demonstrating that perception-driven geometry generation can be made reliable and accessible even on mobile hardware with limited compute.
16:
Overview

Ping Pong Game is a 2D game that creates the illusion of 3D space while keeping the implementation fully in p5.js. The project includes a custom engine, an automated opponent, and a compact gameplay loop built from scratch.

Approach

  • Built the game engine to manage assets, sound, levels, and controls.
  • Used perspective tricks to make the court feel dimensional without leaving the 2D canvas.
  • Added an AI opponent so the game can stand on its own as a complete experience.

Takeaway

The project is a clean example of learning by rebuilding core systems yourself. Instead of relying on a framework, the focus was on understanding how the whole game loop fits together.
17:
Overview

No Job Too Small is a GMTK Game Jam 2024 entry that turns the theme Build to Scale into a physical comedy game about being far too large for the office around you. The result is a chaotic Unity game that landed in the top 1 percent of more than 3,300 entries.

Approach

  • Designed scale as the main mechanic so every object becomes part of the challenge.
  • Used Unity, C#, HLSL, and Blender to build the world, effects, and interactions.
  • Kept the 90s office setting simple so the humor and physics stay readable.

Takeaway

The strongest part of the project is the theme inversion. Instead of treating scale as a visual detail, the game makes scale the actual source of play.
03:
Overview

Drag Task In is a personal schedule assistant built around drag-and-drop planning. It is designed for users who want a fast way to arrange time blocks, keep schedules in sync, and export a clean printable view.

Approach

  • Used React and dnd-kit to make schedule editing feel direct and low-friction.
  • Added FastAPI and Supabase so the app can support sync and persistence.
  • Kept the interface focused on planning rather than dashboard complexity.

Takeaway

The project is about reducing friction in a common productivity workflow. The main design choice was to make the schedule feel editable at a glance instead of forcing users through forms.
25:
Overview

Marvel's Flerken AR Filter is an official AR marketing campaign built around transforming cats into Flerkens for The Marvels. The work combines object detection, visual effects, and cross-platform deployment for a shareable promotional experience.

Approach

  • Built the effect across Lens Studio and Effect House so it could live on multiple platforms.
  • Used JavaScript, HLSL, and object detection to anchor the visual gag to the user's camera feed.
  • Focused on quick recognition and a strong payoff so the filter feels instantly legible.

Takeaway

AR marketing works best when the concept is simple and the transformation lands immediately. This project shows how a good visual hook can carry both technical work and audience engagement.
04:
Overview

A mechanical design project that demonstrates the full CAD-to-fabrication loop: parametric modeling in Fusion 360, tolerance analysis for real-world fit, and iterative prototyping via FDM 3D printing. The goal was a load-bearing curtain hook that clips onto existing hardware without drilling -- a constrained mechanical design problem requiring careful attention to material properties, snap-fit geometry, and physical stress distribution.

Approach

  • Modeled the hook parametrically in Fusion 360 with dimensional constraints tied to standard curtain rod and window frame dimensions.
  • Iterated through multiple print-test-revise cycles, adjusting wall thickness, fillet radii, and snap-fit clearances based on physical testing.
  • Validated load-bearing capacity and long-term deformation behavior under sustained weight -- the same physical validation process used for 3D-printed robotic fixtures and end-effector tooling.

Takeaway

Rapid prototyping is a core robotics skill -- custom brackets, sensor mounts, and end-effector adapters all follow this same loop of CAD modeling, printing, testing, and revising. This project demonstrates hands-on experience with the mechanical design iteration cycle that robotics hardware development depends on.
05:
Overview

Just Another Day is a Unity narrative game made from five mini-games tied together with an emotional storyline. The project uses short gameplay pieces to build toward a stronger ending instead of relying on a single mechanic.

Approach

  • Structured the game around five mini-games so pacing could rise and fall with the story.
  • Used Unity, C#, and Blender to support both gameplay and visual presentation.
  • Focused on keeping the emotional arc clear across the full experience.

Takeaway

The project shows how narrative design can connect otherwise separate gameplay beats. When the story and structure support each other, the ending lands with more weight.
08:
Overview

A Unity XR application for Oculus Quest focused on 6-DOF object grasping, spatial memory, and embodied manipulation. The project implements pick-and-place mechanics with physics-based interactions -- the same interaction paradigm used in robotic manipulation simulation environments like Isaac Sim and MuJoCo, but experienced through a VR headset.

Approach

  • Implemented 6-DOF grasping and placement using Unity's XR Interaction Toolkit with collision detection and physics-based object behavior.
  • Designed spatial memory tasks that require the user to reason about object positions and orientations in 3D -- analogous to workspace awareness in robotic manipulation.
  • Tested across Oculus Quest hardware configurations to ensure stable tracking and consistent interaction at target frame rates.

Takeaway

VR pick-and-place is simulation-adjacent: the same grasp, transport, and release loop appears in robotic manipulation research. Building this in Unity's XR framework provided direct experience with spatial interaction systems, physics constraints, and real-time 3D rendering under performance budgets.
10:
Overview

Creative Coding Experiments is a collection of p5.js sketches that explore generative art and playful simulation. It is less about shipping a single product and more about building intuition through small visual systems.

Approach

  • Used p5.js to prototype randomized grids, patterns, and color studies.
  • Tested ideas quickly so visual direction could emerge through iteration.
  • Treated each sketch as a separate experiment instead of forcing a shared format.

Takeaway

The value of the collection is in the repetition of trying new ideas. Each sketch adds a little more control over motion, composition, and visual rhythm.
09:
Overview

3D Poster Design with Landmarks is a poster series that turns city landmarks into stylized 3D scenes. The work combines Blender modeling with real-world map data and lighting choices that reflect local time of day.

Approach

  • Imported geographic references from Google Maps to anchor each poster in a real place.
  • Built the landmarks in Blender and rendered them in both realistic and stylized formats.
  • Used HDRIs and custom lighting to give each city a distinct atmosphere.

Takeaway

This project sits between data visualization and illustration. The strongest result comes from making the city feel recognizable while still leaving room for artistic interpretation.
10b:
Overview

3D Modeled Teddy Bear is a beginner Blender sculpting exercise focused on making a soft toy feel believable. The project was a chance to practice mesh shaping, subdivision, and material basics in a low-pressure setting.

Approach

  • Used Blender to block out the form and refine the silhouette.
  • Worked through subdivision and surface cleanup to make the model feel softer.
  • Explored basic texturing and shading to finish the piece cleanly.

Takeaway

Simple objects are useful practice because every surface decision is visible. This model helped build the fundamentals that support more complex character and prop work later on.
06:
Overview

No Job Too Small - GMTK Game Jam 2024 is a broader look at the jam build and the rapid production process behind it. The page frames the project as a cross-disciplinary exercise in design, development, and presentation under time pressure.

Approach

  • Kept the scope focused on one playable idea instead of overbuilding the feature set.
  • Drew on a broad toolchain that spans Unity, React, FastAPI, Lens Studio, Three.js, and Blender.
  • Used quick iteration to move from concept to something that could be judged and played.

Takeaway

Jam work rewards clarity. The main lesson is that a strong loop, a readable premise, and disciplined scope usually matter more than raw feature count.
07:
Overview

Portfolio Website is the site itself, built to present projects, skills, and contact points in a way that feels more like a case study than a static gallery. It serves as both a personal brand site and a living example of the front-end work behind the portfolio.

Approach

  • Structured the site around project discovery so the best work is easy to browse.
  • Kept the layout responsive and content-driven so it can support different project types.
  • Used the same system to balance design polish, maintainability, and practical editing.

Takeaway

The portfolio is part showcase and part product. It needs to explain the work clearly while also proving that the designer can ship a coherent interface end to end.
18:
Overview

Spaceman Rescue is a space-themed adventure project that combines storytelling, puzzle solving, and 3D production work. The goal was to make the world feel immersive while still keeping the gameplay easy to follow.

Approach

  • Used Unity to hold the gameplay together while Blender handled the 3D asset work.
  • Designed puzzles and interactions to support the narrative instead of interrupting it.
  • Balanced visual ambition with a clear play loop so the experience stays readable.

Takeaway

The project reflects the value of aligning story and mechanics. When the environment, puzzles, and narrative point in the same direction, the game feels more cohesive.
19:
Overview

VR Magic is a virtual reality experience built around magical interactions, spatial audio, and visual effects. The project explores how presence and feedback can make a fantasy system feel physical.

Approach

  • Built the experience in Unity with C# and VR-specific interaction patterns.
  • Used hand tracking and motion-driven feedback to make spellcasting feel responsive.
  • Layered in visual effects and audio cues to strengthen the illusion of magic.

Takeaway

The project shows that VR magic works when the feedback loop is immediate. If the user can see, hear, and feel the effect at once, the interaction reads as believable.
20:
Overview

Dragon Roaster is an early fantasy game about training and battling dragons. It combines 3D modeling, visual effects, and Unity development into a project that was as much about learning the pipeline as it was about the final game loop.

Approach

  • Built the core experience in Unity with C#.
  • Used Blender to support the characters and environments.
  • Focused on combat, progression, and atmosphere as the main pillars of the game.

Takeaway

Early projects are useful because they force you to connect art, code, and design in one place. This one helped establish the foundation for later gameplay and 3D work.
21:
Overview

VR Chess is a virtual reality version of chess that lets the player walk around the board, pick up pieces, and experience a familiar game in a more embodied way. The project turns a classic interface into a spatial one.

Approach

  • Built the experience in Unity with C# and VR interaction logic.
  • Designed the board so the player can see it from any angle and physically move around it.
  • Used spatial audio and haptic feedback to make actions feel grounded.

Takeaway

The project is a strong example of how a known game can become fresh again when the interface changes. The rules stay the same, but the physicality changes the experience completely.
22:
Overview

3D Snake Game is a reimagining of the classic snake formula in 3D space. It keeps the familiar core loop but adds a stronger sense of spatial awareness through movement, obstacles, and progression.

Approach

  • Built the game in Unity with C#.
  • Adapted the snake controls so they work in a 3D environment instead of a flat grid.
  • Added power-ups, obstacles, and level progression to keep the pace changing.

Takeaway

The project is a good example of taking a simple mechanic and changing the camera, space, and movement to make it feel new. The core idea remains recognizable, but the challenge becomes more dimensional.
24:
Overview

My Shader Projects is a collection of shader experiments that explore rendering techniques, visual effects, and math-driven animation. The page acts as a sketchbook for graphics work across ShaderToy and related tools.

Approach

  • Used shader code to explore light, motion, and procedural pattern generation.
  • Worked through multiple experiments instead of trying to force a single visual style.
  • Focused on the relationship between formula, timing, and the resulting image.

Takeaway

This collection is about learning through visual iteration. Shaders reward precision, but they also leave room for unexpected results when the math is pushed in the right way.
